perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means any information disclosed (whether before or after the date of this Agr eement, in writing, verbally or otherwise and whether directly or indirectly) by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party or an Affiliate of the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Pa rty in connection with the Purpose;
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information means any information disclosed (whether before or after the date of this Agr eement, in writing, verbally or otherwise and whether directly or indirectly) by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Pa rty in connection with the Purpose, **provided that such information is clearly marked as 'Confidential' at the time of disclosure**;
perturbation.explanation: This change adds a condition that Confidential Information must be marked as 'Confidential' at the time of disclosure. This contradicts later sections, such as 8.1, which mandates the return or destruction of 'all' Confidential Information upon request without explicitly accounting for unmarked information. The added condition creates uncertainty regarding information disclosed verbally or visually that was not labeled as confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each party undertakes within three business days of the receipt of a written request of the other party or on termination of this Agreement, at the option of the other party: (a) to return to the other party all of the other party's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its po ssession, custody or control that bear or incorporate any pa rt of the other pa rty's Confidential Information; or (b) to destroy by shredding or incineration all of the other pa rty's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which bear or inco rporate any part of the other party's Confidential Information and take reason able steps to expung e all Confidential Information (and any copy that may have been ma de) from any computer, word processor or other device containing the Confidential Information. Any destruction of Confidential Information will be certified in writing to the other party by an authorised officer supervising it.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a condition that Confidential Information must be marked as 'Confidential' at the time of disclosure. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which mandates the return or destruction of 'all' Confidential Information upon request, without accounting for unmarked information. This creates uncertainty regarding the handling of verbally or visually disclosed information not explicitly labeled as confidential, as described in the explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Purpose means discussions and negotiations betw een the parties in relation to the supply of IT infrastructure and services and the associated tender exercise;
perturbation.changed_text: Purpose means discussions and negotiations betw een the parties in relation to the supply of IT infrastructure and services and the associated tender exercise; **however, the specific scope of IT infrastructure and services is limited to hardware components only.**
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Purpose' is broad, encompassing IT infrastructure and services. The change narrows the definition to only include hardware components. This creates a contradiction because other sections of the agreement likely assume a broader scope of IT services, such as Clause 4.1(b), which refers to professional advisors who would likely handle more than just hardware.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each party may disclose th e Confidential Information of the other party in the following circumstances: (a) with the prior written cons ent of the other party; (b) to its employees, professional advisors, affiliates, consultants, shareholders, financiers and authorised representa tives but only to the extent that disclosure is necessary for the purpose; or (c) where disclosure is required by law, by a court of competen t jurisdiction, by the rules of any stock exchange or by another appropriate regulatory body, provided that all reasonable steps to pr event such disclosure shall be taken, the disclosure shall be of the minimum amount re quired, and the Receiving Party consults the Disclosing Party first on the proposed form, timing, nature and purpose of the disclosure with as much prior notice as is practicable in the circumstances.
perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the definition of 'Purpose' to only include hardware components, contradicting the original broad definition encompassing IT infrastructure and services. This creates a conflict with the contradicted text, Clause (b), which allows disclosure of confidential information to professional advisors. Since the revised 'Purpose' is limited to hardware, disclosing information to professional advisors for broader IT services contradicts this new, narrower definition, as these advisors might not only deal with hardware, as supported by the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall, in relation to the Conf idential Information of the other party, exercise in relation to the Confidential Information no lesser security measures and degree of care than those wh ich it applies to its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall, in relation to the Conf idential Information of the other party, exercise in relation to the Confidential Information no lesser security measures and degree of care than those wh ich it applies to its own confidential information; **provided, however, that no party shall be liable for security breaches caused by third-party cloud storage providers.**
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces a contradiction concerning responsibility for security measures. The original text mandates each party to maintain a security level equivalent to what they apply to their own confidential data. The added clause, however, absolves them of liability for breaches caused by third-party cloud providers. This contradicts the original clause, leading to uncertainty about whether a party is fully responsible for protecting Confidential Information if they use cloud storage. Especially if they claim to be using the same level of security as their own confidential data.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each party shall, in relation to the Conf idential Information of the other party, exercise in relation to the Confidential Information no lesser security measures and degree of care than those wh ich it applies to its own confidential information; provided, however, that no party shall be liable for security breaches caused by third-party cloud storage providers.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a clause that absolves parties from liability for security breaches caused by third-party cloud storage providers, despite the original text requiring them to exercise the same degree of care as they do for their own confidential information. This creates a direct conflict, as the 'no liability' clause contradicts the 'same degree of care' obligation. The contradiction is in the same location, as the new changes directly affect the previous section by introducing an exception to the rule.

perturbation.original_text: Each party undertakes within three business days of the receipt of a written request of the other party or on termination of this Agreement, at the option of the other party: (a) to return to the other party all of the other party's Confidential Information
perturbation.changed_text: Each party undertakes within three business days of the receipt of a written request of the other party or on termination of this Agreement, at the option of the other party: (a) to return to the other party all of the other party's Confidential Information, **except any automatically generated backups**
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement requires the return of all Confidential Information upon request or termination. The changed text introduces an exception for 'automatically generated backups.' This directly contradicts the 'all' inclusive requirement. It is unclear whether backups are still considered confidential information that need to be protected.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each party undertakes within three business days of the receipt of a written request of the other party or on termination of this Agreement, at the option of the other party: (a) to return to the other party all of the other party's Confidential Information, except any automatically generated backups and that part of all documents and other material in its po ssession, custody or control that bear or incorporate any pa rt of the other pa rty's Confidential Information; or (b) to destroy by shredding or incineration all of the other pa rty's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which bear or inco rporate any part of the other party's Confidential Information and take reason able steps to expung e all Confidential Information (and any copy that may have been ma de) from any computer, word processor or other device containing the Confidential Information. Any destruction of Confidential Information will be certified in writing to the other party by an authorised officer supervising it.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception ('except any automatically generated backups') to the requirement to return 'all' Confidential Information. This exception directly contradicts the initial 'all' inclusive requirement in both the original text and the contradicted text. The modified text adds an exclusion that creates uncertainty about what constitutes Confidential Information and whether backups are exempt from the obligations regarding confidential information, thus causing contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any claim for lo sses under this Agreement sha ll be restricted to direct loss only and shall not extend to indirect or cons equential loss.
perturbation.changed_text: Any claim for lo sses under this Agreement sha ll be restricted to direct loss only and shall not extend to indirect or cons equential loss; **however, in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct, the limitation on indirect or consequential loss shall not apply.**
perturbation.explanation: The original clause limits liability to direct losses, excluding indirect and consequential losses. The added exception allows for claims of indirect or consequential losses in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct. This creates an in-text contradiction that complicates how potential liabilities are assessed and could lead to disputes about what constitutes 'gross negligence' or 'willful misconduct'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: It is acknowledged that damages would no t be an adequate remedy for a breach of this Agreement and each party is entitled to the re medy of injunction, specific performance and other equitable relief for a threatened or actual breach of this Agreement. Any claim for lo sses under this Agreement sha ll be restricted to direct loss only and shall not extend to indirect or cons equential loss; however, in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct, the limitation on indirect or consequential loss shall not apply.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that any claim for losses under the agreement shall be restricted to direct loss only and shall not extend to indirect or consequential loss. The changed text adds an exception, stating that in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct, the limitation on indirect or consequential loss shall not apply. The contradicted text includes the changed text and therefore contradicts itself, as it initially restricts claims to direct loss only but then allows claims for indirect or consequential loss under specific circumstances. This creates a clear and direct conflict within the document regarding the types of losses that can be claimed, leading to an in-text contradiction as per the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: A notice or other communication under or in connection with th is Agreement shall be in writing, in Eng lish and shall be delive red personally or sent by fax, to the party due to receive the no tice or communication at its address set out in this Agreement or another address as specified by that party by wr itten notice to the other.
perturbation.changed_text: A notice or other communication under or in connection with th is Agreement shall be in writing, in Eng lish and shall be delive red personally or sent by fax, to the party due to receive the no tice or communication at its address set out in this Agreement or another address as specified by that party by wr itten notice to the other. **However, for routine operational matters, email communication is acceptable.**
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement specifies that all notices must be in writing, either delivered personally or sent by fax. The added sentence introduces email as an acceptable form of communication for 'routine operational matters.' This creates ambiguity and in-text contradiction because the agreement does not define 'routine operational matters', leading to disputes about which communications require formal notice and which can be sent by email. This is especially problematic if a crucial notice is sent via email and then challenged due to not adhering to the written communication requirement of the main clause.
perturbation.contradicted_text: A notice or other communication under or in connection with th is Agreement shall be in writing, in Eng lish and shall be delive red personally or sent by fax, to the party due to receive the no tice or communication at its address set out in this Agreement or another address as specified by that party by wr itten notice to the other. However, for routine operational matters, email communication is acceptable.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that all notices or communications under the agreement be in writing and delivered personally or via fax. The changed text introduces an exception, allowing email communication for 'routine operational matters'. This creates a direct contradiction because the agreement doesn't define 'routine operational matters', leading to uncertainty about which communications require formal written notice versus email, directly conflicting with the original requirement for all notices to be in writing.
